name: Human Biology — Complete Illustrated Bank
description: 260 illustrated MCQs across 8 topics: cell, tissues, histology, cell division, fertilization, cardiovascular, respiratory and digestive systems.
author: MJ Technology Solutions

questions.question: The study of the structure and function of cells is called:
questions.explanation: Cytology (cell biology) is the study of the structure and function of cells.

questions.question: Which term best describes the cell?
questions.explanation: The cell is the body's smallest functional (living) unit, capable of maintaining homeostasis.
questions.imageAlt: Structure of a eukaryotic (animal) cell

questions.question: Loss of a cell's ability to maintain homeostasis most directly leads to:
questions.explanation: Homeostasis maintains internal balance; losing it results in disease.

questions.question: The three main parts of the human cell are the plasma membrane, the nucleus and the:
questions.explanation: The cell's three main parts are the plasma membrane, cytoplasm and nucleus.
questions.imageAlt: Fully labelled animal cell showing organelles

questions.question: The currently accepted model describing the structure of the plasma membrane is the:
questions.explanation: The fluid-mosaic model describes the membrane as a fluid lipid bilayer with mosaic of proteins.
questions.imageAlt: Fluid-mosaic model of the plasma membrane

questions.question: The plasma membrane is chiefly composed of:
options: Lipids, proteins and a small amount of carbohydrate
questions.explanation: The membrane is made of lipids, proteins and a small amount of carbohydrate.

questions.question: In a phospholipid molecule, the head region is:
questions.explanation: The phospholipid head is electrically charged and hydrophilic (water-loving).
questions.imageAlt: A single phospholipid molecule: hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail

questions.question: In the phospholipid bilayer, the hydrophobic tails:
options: Face the watery surfaces on both sides
Point outward toward the extracellular fluid
questions.explanation: The hydrophobic tails point inward, forming a central water-repelling layer between the two hydrophilic surfaces.
questions.imageAlt: Phospholipid bilayer arranged around water

questions.question: Membrane proteins that span the entire lipid bilayer and protrude on both sides are called:
questions.explanation: Transmembrane integral proteins span the whole bilayer, protruding into cytosol and extracellular fluid.
questions.imageAlt: Integral (transmembrane) and peripheral membrane proteins

questions.question: Peripheral membrane proteins are best described as:
options: Loosely attached to the polar heads of lipids or to integral proteins
Firmly embedded through the whole bilayer
questions.explanation: Peripheral proteins are not firmly embedded; they attach to lipid polar heads or to integral proteins at the surface.

questions.question: A carbohydrate chain attached to a membrane protein forms a:
questions.explanation: Carbohydrate linked to protein forms a glycoprotein; linked to lipid it forms a glycolipid.

questions.question: Membrane carbohydrates that attach as branched molecules give the cell its:
questions.explanation: Branched carbohydrate molecules provide the immunological identity of the cell.

questions.question: Which is NOT a function of the plasma membrane?
options: Selective control of substances entering and leaving
Acting as a receptor for hormones
questions.explanation: Ribosomal RNA is produced by the nucleolus, not the plasma membrane.

questions.question: The fluid portion of the cytoplasm surrounding the organelles is the:
questions.explanation: Cytosol is the fluid portion of cytoplasm surrounding the organelles.

questions.question: Approximately what fraction of total cell volume is the cytosol?
questions.explanation: The cytosol constitutes about 55% of the total cell volume.

questions.question: The network of protein filaments extending throughout the cytosol is the:
questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton is a network of protein filaments that maintains cell shape and enables movement.
questions.imageAlt: Cytoskeleton: microfilaments, intermediate filaments and microtubules

questions.question: Microfilaments of the cytoskeleton are made of the protein:
questions.explanation: Microfilaments are long, thin elements made of the protein actin.

questions.question: The largest cytoskeletal components, hollow tubes made of tubulin, are the:
questions.explanation: Microtubules are the largest cytoskeletal components — hollow tubes made of tubulin (~25 nm).

questions.question: Which cytoskeletal element has a diameter (~10 nm) between that of microtubules and microfilaments?
questions.explanation: Intermediate filaments (~10 nm) have diameters between microtubules (25 nm) and microfilaments (8 nm).

questions.question: The largest organelle and the control centre of the cell is the:
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the largest organelle and directs all metabolic activities of the cell.
questions.imageAlt: Nucleus showing nuclear envelope, pores, chromatin and nucleolus

questions.question: Ribosomal RNA, needed to make ribosomes, is produced by the:
questions.explanation: The small, round nucleolus produces ribosomal RNA, which is used to make ribosomes.

questions.question: The thin fibres of DNA and protein within the nucleus are known as:
questions.explanation: DNA combined with protein forms thin fibres called chromatin inside the nucleus.

questions.question: The nuclear envelope allows materials to move in and out through structures called:
questions.explanation: Nuclear pores in the porous nuclear envelope allow ribosomes and other materials to pass.

questions.question: The organelle described as the 'powerhouse' of the cell is the:
questions.explanation: Mitochondria generate most of the cell's chemical energy through aerobic respiration.
questions.imageAlt: Mitochondrion showing cristae, matrix and inner/outer membranes

questions.question: The folds of the inner mitochondrial membrane, which increase surface area, are called:
questions.explanation: The inner mitochondrial membrane folds into cristae, increasing the surface area for respiration.

questions.question: Which cells would be expected to contain the largest number of mitochondria?
questions.explanation: Highly active cells such as muscle, liver and kidney cells contain many mitochondria.

questions.question: The site of protein synthesis in the cell is the:
questions.explanation: Ribosomes, tiny granules of RNA and protein, are the site of protein synthesis.
questions.imageAlt: Ribosome subunits and rough endoplasmic reticulum

questions.question: Free ribosomes in the cytoplasm mainly synthesise proteins that are:
questions.explanation: Free ribosomes make proteins for use within the cell; membrane-bound ribosomes make proteins for export.

questions.question: Rough endoplasmic reticulum is distinguished from smooth ER because it:
options: Has no connection to the nucleus
questions.explanation: Rough ER is studded with ribosomes and synthesises proteins; smooth ER lacks ribosomes.

questions.question: Smooth endoplasmic reticulum is chiefly involved in the synthesis of:
questions.explanation: Smooth ER contains unique enzymes and synthesises fatty acids and steroids.

questions.question: Which organelle modifies, packages and transports proteins received from the ER?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus modifies, packages and transports proteins and lipids in vesicles.
questions.imageAlt: Golgi apparatus with cis and trans faces and vesicles

questions.question: The Golgi apparatus consists of:
questions.explanation: The Golgi is made of stacks of closely folded, flattened membranous sacs (cisternae).

questions.question: Which organelle contains digestive enzymes that break down organelle fragments and large molecules?
questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain hydrolytic enzymes that digest organelle fragments and large molecules.
questions.imageAlt: Lysosome containing hydrolytic enzymes

questions.question: Lysosomes are formed from the:
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are vesicles that bud from the Golgi complex.

questions.question: Centrosomes, which are important in cell division, are composed of a pair of:
questions.explanation: A centrosome consists of a pair of centrioles, cylindrical structures made of microtubules.
questions.imageAlt: A pair of centrioles

questions.question: Centrioles are cylindrical structures built from:
questions.explanation: Centrioles are cylindrical structures made up of microtubules.

questions.question: Compared with cilia, flagella are typically:
questions.explanation: Flagella are much longer, single whip-like projections; cilia are numerous and short.
questions.imageAlt: Cell surface projections: cilia and flagellum

questions.question: Cilia lining the fallopian tubes function to:
options: Move the ovum toward the uterus
questions.explanation: Cilia move fluids along a surface — e.g. sweeping the ovum toward the uterus.

questions.question: The whip-like flagellum is best exemplified in the human body by the:
questions.explanation: A flagellum moves an entire cell — the classic example is the tail of a sperm.

questions.question: Both cilia and flagella are motile projections constructed of:
questions.explanation: Cilia and flagella are motile surface projections that consist of microtubules.

questions.question: The human body is composed of how many basic types of tissue?
questions.explanation: The four basic tissue types are epithelial, connective, muscular and nervous tissue.
questions.imageAlt: The four basic tissue types

questions.question: Which sequence correctly shows increasing levels of structural organisation?
questions.explanation: Cells group into tissues, tissues into organs, and organs into organ systems.
questions.imageAlt: Levels of organisation from cells to body systems

questions.question: A key feature that distinguishes epithelial tissue is that it is:
options: Avascular and rests on a basement membrane
questions.explanation: Epithelium is avascular (no blood vessels), nourished by diffusion, and rests on a basement membrane.

questions.question: Epithelium consisting of only a single layer of cells is described as:
questions.explanation: Simple epithelium contains only one layer of cells; stratified has more than one.
questions.imageAlt: Classification of epithelial tissue by layers and cell shape

questions.question: Flat, thin cells whose only bulge is the nucleus form which epithelium?
questions.explanation: Simple squamous epithelium is a single layer of flat, thin cells ideal for diffusion.

questions.question: Cells that are roughly as tall as they are wide characterise which epithelium?
questions.explanation: Simple cuboidal cells are about as tall as they are wide (e.g. renal collecting tubule, pancreatic duct).
questions.imageAlt: Simple cuboidal epithelium

questions.question: Simple columnar epithelium, specialised for absorption, is characterised by cells that are:
questions.explanation: Simple columnar cells are taller than they are wide and often bear microvilli for absorption.

questions.question: Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium is found mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium forms the epidermis of the skin.

questions.question: The epithelium of the urinary bladder that changes shape as the organ distends is:
questions.explanation: Transitional epithelium (urothelium) has dome-like surface (umbrella) cells that change with distention.

questions.question: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium characteristically lines the:
questions.explanation: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium lines passages of the upper respiratory tract.

questions.question: A goblet cell is an example of a(n):
questions.explanation: A goblet cell is a unicellular gland — a large isolated secretory cell.

questions.question: Glands that convey their secretions directly into the bloodstream are:
questions.explanation: Endocrine glands are ductless and release secretions into the bloodstream; exocrine glands use ducts.
questions.imageAlt: Gland duct shapes: tubular, acinar and alveolar

questions.question: The three components of connective tissue are cells, ground substance and:
questions.explanation: Connective tissue consists of cells, fibres and ground substance.

questions.question: Which connective-tissue cell is chiefly responsible for synthesising the extracellular matrix?
questions.explanation: Fibroblasts synthesise the extracellular matrix (fibres and ground substance).

questions.question: Ground substance is a gel largely made of large molecules called:
questions.explanation: Ground substance consists of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) that link to form larger proteoglycans.

questions.question: Which fibre type provides resistance to stretching and contains type I collagen?
questions.explanation: Collagen fibres contain type I collagen and resist stretching.

questions.question: Elastic fibres owe their stretch-and-recoil property to the proteins:
questions.explanation: Elastic fibres contain elastin and fibrillin, allowing stretch and return to original length.

questions.question: Reticular fibres are composed of:
questions.explanation: Reticular fibres contain type III collagen and have a supportive function.

questions.question: Loose (areolar) connective tissue is characterised by an abundance of:
options: Fibroblasts and macrophages with loosely arranged fibres
questions.explanation: Areolar tissue has many fibroblasts and macrophages with loosely arranged collagen, elastic and reticular fibres.
questions.imageAlt: Loose (areolar) connective tissue

questions.question: Tendons and ligaments, with collagen fibres in a definite parallel pattern, are examples of:
questions.explanation: Dense regular connective tissue has collagen arranged in a definite (parallel) pattern — as in tendons and ligaments.

questions.question: Which adipose tissue contains cells with multiple lipid droplets and abundant mitochondria for thermogenesis?
questions.explanation: Brown adipose tissue has multiple lipid droplets and many mitochondria for heat production (thermogenesis).

questions.question: The cells that secrete the extracellular matrix of cartilage are the:
questions.explanation: Chondrocytes secrete the extracellular matrix of cartilage.

questions.question: The most common type of cartilage, found in joints, trachea and bronchi, is:
questions.explanation: Hyaline cartilage is the most common type, found in joints, larynx, trachea and bronchi.

questions.question: Which cartilage, rich in elastic fibres, forms the external ear and epiglottis?
questions.explanation: Elastic cartilage, rich in elastic fibres, forms the external ear and epiglottis.

questions.question: The intervertebral discs and symphysis pubis are composed of:
questions.explanation: Fibrocartilage, with its dense collagen network, forms intervertebral discs and the symphysis pubis.

questions.question: Multinucleated giant cells that resorb and remodel bone tissue are:
questions.explanation: Osteoclasts are multinucleated giant cells involved in the resorption and remodeling of bone.

questions.question: Bone cells that synthesise the organic components of the bone matrix are:
questions.explanation: Osteoblasts synthesise the organic components of the bone matrix.

questions.question: The functional unit of compact bone, made of lamellae surrounding a central canal, is the:
questions.explanation: The osteon (Haversian system) — concentric lamellae around a Haversian canal — is the unit of compact bone.
questions.imageAlt: Structure of an osteon in compact bone

questions.question: In compact bone, horizontal channels that connect Haversian canals are called:
questions.explanation: Volkmann's canals run horizontally, connecting Haversian canals and transmitting vessels from the periosteum.

questions.question: Which feature is characteristic of spongy (rather than compact) bone?
questions.explanation: Spongy bone forms a honeycomb of trabeculae and lacks Haversian and Volkmann's canals.

questions.question: Blood is classified as a specialised type of which tissue?
questions.explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue with plasma as its matrix and formed elements as its cells.
questions.imageAlt: Blood cells: red cells, white cells and platelets

questions.question: In blood, the liquid matrix (plasma) makes up approximately what percentage?
questions.explanation: Plasma (the liquid matrix) forms about 55% of blood; formed elements make up about 45%.

questions.question: The two cell types that make up nervous tissue are neurones and:
questions.explanation: Nervous tissue consists of neurones and supporting glial cells.
questions.imageAlt: Structure of a neurone

questions.question: The part of a neurone that carries impulses AWAY from the cell body is the:
questions.explanation: The axon conveys impulses away from the cell body; dendrites carry impulses toward it.

questions.question: The gaps in the myelin sheath along an axon are called the:
questions.explanation: Nodes of Ranvier are the gaps between myelin sheaths along an axon.

questions.question: Which glial cells form the blood–brain barrier?
questions.explanation: Astrocytes help form the blood–brain barrier.
questions.imageAlt: Types of glial cells

questions.question: Myelin in the central nervous system is produced by:
questions.explanation: Oligodendrocytes synthesise myelin in the CNS; microglia are phagocytic.

questions.question: Which muscle type is non-striated, spindle-shaped, with a single central nucleus?
questions.explanation: Smooth muscle is non-striated, spindle-shaped and involuntary with one central nucleus.

questions.question: Intercalated discs are a distinguishing feature of which muscle type?
questions.explanation: Intercalated discs join cardiac muscle cells and are unique to cardiac muscle.
questions.imageAlt: Cardiac muscle fibres with intercalated discs

questions.question: Long, cylindrical, multinucleated fibres with striations, under voluntary control, are found in:
questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle fibres are long, cylindrical, multinucleated, striated and voluntary.

questions.question: Histology is best defined as the study of:
questions.explanation: Histology is the study of normal tissues and their structure.

questions.question: The study of how disease processes affect tissues is called:
questions.explanation: Histopathology examines how disease processes affect tissues for diagnosis.

questions.question: Which is the correct order of the four steps in standard tissue preparation?
questions.explanation: Standard preparation proceeds: fixation → embedding → sectioning → staining.

questions.question: The step that stabilises and preserves tissue, halting decay, is:
questions.explanation: Fixation stabilises and preserves the tissue; routine fixation uses formaldehyde (formalin).

questions.question: The chemical most commonly used for routine tissue fixation is:
questions.explanation: Routine fixation uses a buffered formaldehyde (formalin) solution to stabilise cell structures.

questions.question: During processing, tissue is dehydrated by passing it through increasing concentrations of:
questions.explanation: Dehydration replaces water with increasing concentrations of ethyl alcohol (0 to 100%).

questions.question: Tissue is routinely embedded in which solid medium to allow thin sectioning?
questions.explanation: Tissue is embedded in paraffin wax, which hardens to support very thin sectioning.

questions.question: The paraffin solvent used because wax is not soluble in water or alcohol is:
questions.explanation: Xylene, miscible with alcohol, is used to clear the tissue before paraffin infiltration.

questions.question: The instrument used to cut very thin tissue sections is the:
questions.explanation: Sectioning — cutting very thin slices — is performed with a microtome.
questions.imageAlt: A microtome used for sectioning tissue

questions.question: The most widely used routine histology stain is:
questions.explanation: Haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) is by far the most widely used routine histological stain.
questions.imageAlt: Trachea stained with H&E

questions.question: Haematoxylin is a basic stain that colours structures:
questions.explanation: Haematoxylin is a basic stain giving a deep purple/blue colour.

questions.question: Structures that stain strongly with haematoxylin, such as cell nuclei, are described as:
questions.explanation: Structures that bind basic stains (e.g. nuclei, ribosomes) are basophilic and stain purple with H&E.

questions.question: Eosin is an acidic stain that colours acidophilic structures:
questions.explanation: Eosin, an acidic stain, colours acidophilic structures such as collagen and RBCs red/pink.

questions.question: With H&E, an unexpected yellow or brown colour most likely indicates:
options: An intrinsic pigment such as melanin or lipofuscin
questions.explanation: Colours other than purple/blue and red/pink usually reflect an intrinsic pigment such as melanin or lipofuscin.

questions.question: To distinguish collagen (blue) from smooth muscle (red), which stain is used?
questions.explanation: Trichrome stain colours collagen blue/green and smooth muscle red, distinguishing the two.
questions.imageAlt: Artery stained with trichrome: collagen blue, smooth muscle red

questions.question: The Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain is particularly useful for demonstrating:
questions.explanation: PAS stains glycoproteins and glycogen (and basement membranes).
questions.imageAlt: PAS stain highlighting glycogen

questions.question: Sudan black is a dye used to stain which tissue component?
questions.explanation: Sudan black stains lipid-containing structures (e.g. myelin) a brownish-black colour.

questions.question: Compared with H&E and special stains, immunohistochemistry (IHC) is:
options: Directed to a specific protein marker
questions.explanation: IHC is specific — it detects particular protein markers, unlike non-specific H&E/special stains.
questions.imageAlt: Immunohistochemistry detection system

questions.question: In immunohistochemistry, the first reagent applied binds specifically to the target antigen; it is the:
questions.explanation: The primary antibody is applied first and binds specifically to the target antigen.

questions.question: In IHC, the brown-staining chromogen commonly used for most applications is:
questions.explanation: DAB is oxidised (via horseradish peroxidase) to form a strong, permanent brown precipitate.

questions.question: Cell division that produces daughter cells genetically IDENTICAL to the parent cell is:
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell.

questions.question: Meiosis differs from mitosis in that it produces daughter cells with:
options: Half the number of chromosomes (haploid gametes)
questions.explanation: Meiosis halves the chromosome number, producing haploid gametes.
questions.imageAlt: Mitosis versus meiosis: daughter cells have half the chromosomes

questions.question: The constricted region of a chromosome to which spindle microtubules attach is the:
questions.explanation: The centromere is the constricted region bearing kinetochores for microtubule attachment.
questions.imageAlt: Chromosome showing centromere, kinetochore and sister chromatids

questions.question: Protein discs bound to the centromere that serve as attachment points for microtubules are:
questions.explanation: Kinetochores are protein discs at the centromere where spindle microtubules attach.

questions.question: During which phase of the cell cycle is DNA replicated?
questions.explanation: DNA replication occurs during the S (synthesis) phase of interphase.
questions.imageAlt: Phases of the cell cycle

questions.question: Collectively, the G1, S and G2 phases make up:
questions.explanation: Interphase comprises G1, S and G2, during which the cell grows and replicates its DNA.

questions.question: In the G1 phase, the cell replicates organelles and cytosolic components but NOT its:
questions.explanation: G1 is metabolically active and replicates organelles, but DNA is not replicated until S phase.

questions.question: The correct order of the four mitotic phases is:
questions.explanation: Mitosis proceeds: prophase → metaphase → anaphase → telophase.

questions.question: During which phase do the nuclear membrane break down and the mitotic spindle begin to form?
questions.explanation: In prophase, chromatin condenses, the nuclear membrane breaks down and the spindle begins forming.
questions.imageAlt: Prophase of mitosis

questions.question: The alignment of chromosomes along the centre of the spindle occurs during:
questions.explanation: In metaphase, chromosomes align on the metaphase plate at the centre of the spindle.
questions.imageAlt: Metaphase of mitosis

questions.question: The midpoint region where chromosomes line up in metaphase is called the:
questions.explanation: The chromosomes align at the midpoint region called the metaphase plate.

questions.question: During anaphase, the centromeres split and:
options: Chromosomes line up in the middle
Sister chromatids move toward opposite poles
questions.explanation: In anaphase the centromeres split and the separated chromatids are pulled to opposite poles.
questions.imageAlt: Anaphase of mitosis

questions.question: During which phase does the nuclear envelope reform and the nucleoli reappear?
questions.explanation: In telophase the spindle disappears, chromosomes uncoil, and the nuclear envelope and nucleoli reappear.
questions.imageAlt: Telophase of mitosis

questions.question: The division of the cytoplasm and organelles into two daughter cells is called:
questions.explanation: Cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm and organelles, separating the cell into two daughter cells.

questions.question: Crossing over, which produces genetic recombination, occurs during:
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs in prophase I of meiosis, when non-sister chromatids exchange genetic material.
questions.imageAlt: Crossing over between non-sister chromatids

questions.question: The exchange of genetic material during crossing over is chiefly responsible for:
questions.explanation: Crossing over produces genetic recombination, responsible for genetic variation among humans.

questions.question: The pairing of homologous chromosomes in prophase I is known as:
questions.explanation: In prophase I, homologous chromosomes pair together in a process called synapsis.

questions.question: A key difference in anaphase I of meiosis compared with mitosis is that:
questions.explanation: In anaphase I, homologous chromosomes separate but the centromeres do not split.
questions.imageAlt: Meiotic spindle and metaphase plate

questions.question: After telophase I of meiosis, each resulting cell contains:
questions.explanation: After telophase I, each cell now contains a haploid number of chromosomes.

questions.question: The phases of meiosis II are most similar to those of:
questions.explanation: The phases of meiosis II closely resemble those of mitosis.
questions.imageAlt: Comparison of mitosis and meiosis

questions.question: Fertilization occurs when a sperm and an oocyte combine and their nuclei:
questions.explanation: Fertilization occurs when a sperm and oocyte combine and their haploid nuclei fuse.

questions.question: The single cell formed immediately after the fusion of sperm and egg is called the:
questions.explanation: The fused sperm and egg form a diploid single cell called the zygote.

questions.question: A zygote is a diploid cell, meaning it contains:
options: Twice the normal number of chromosomes
A full set of chromosomes (46 in humans)
questions.explanation: Combining two haploid gametes forms a diploid zygote with the full 46 chromosomes.

questions.question: In which anatomical location does fertilization of the ovum normally occur?
questions.explanation: Fertilization normally occurs in the ampulla of the uterine tube.
questions.imageAlt: Fertilization in the fallopian tube and journey to the uterus

questions.question: The whole fertilization process lasts approximately:
questions.explanation: Fertilization is completed within about 24 hours of ovulation.

questions.question: Which enzyme, released from the sperm acrosome, helps it penetrate the corona radiata?
questions.explanation: Hyaluronidase from the acrosome helps the sperm penetrate the corona radiata.
questions.imageAlt: Structure of a sperm cell

questions.question: The enzymes acrosin and neuraminidase enable the sperm to penetrate the:
questions.explanation: Acrosin and neuraminidase cause lysis of the zona pellucida, forming a path to the oocyte.
questions.imageAlt: Structure of the human egg (ovum) with zona pellucida and corona radiata

questions.question: After one sperm penetrates, the change that makes the zona pellucida impermeable to other sperm is the:
questions.explanation: The zona reaction makes the zona pellucida impermeable to additional sperm, preventing polyspermy.

questions.question: Entry of the sperm triggers the secondary oocyte to complete its:
questions.explanation: After sperm entry, the secondary oocyte completes its second meiotic division, forming the ovum and a polar body.

questions.question: The nucleus of the mature oocyte after sperm entry is known as the:
questions.explanation: The nucleus of the mature oocyte becomes the female pronucleus.

questions.question: Each pronucleus contains how many chromosomes before they fuse?
questions.explanation: Each pronucleus has 23 chromosomes, so the zygote ends up with 46 in total.

questions.question: Which is NOT one of the main results of fertilization?
options: Determination of the sex of the embryo
Restoration of the diploid chromosome number
questions.explanation: Fertilization restores diploidy, determines sex and initiates cleavage; placenta forms later from the trophoblast.

questions.question: The mitotic divisions that increase cell number without increasing overall size are called:
questions.explanation: Cleavage divisions increase cell number while the overall size stays the same.
questions.imageAlt: Cleavage: 2-cell, 4-cell, morula and blastocyst stages

questions.question: The individual cells produced by cleavage of the zygote are known as:
questions.explanation: The cells produced by cleavage are called blastomeres.

questions.question: A solid ball of cells (about the 32-cell stage) with an inner and outer cell mass is the:
questions.explanation: At the 32-cell stage the embryo is a morula — a solid ball with inner cell mass and trophoblast.

questions.question: The outer cell mass (trophoblast) of the developing embryo will eventually form part of the:
questions.explanation: The trophoblast (outer cell mass) becomes part of the placenta; the inner cell mass becomes the embryo.

questions.question: During gastrulation, how many germ cell layers are formed?
questions.explanation: Gastrulation forms three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm.
questions.imageAlt: Blastula and gastrula with the three germ layers

questions.question: The nervous system and the epidermis of the skin develop from which germ layer?
questions.explanation: The ectoderm (outer layer) forms the nervous system and epidermis of the skin.

questions.question: Bones, muscles, blood and blood vessels develop mainly from which germ layer?
questions.explanation: The mesoderm (middle layer) forms bones, muscles, blood, blood vessels and the reproductive/excretory systems.

questions.question: Implantation of the blastocyst into the endometrium begins around:
questions.explanation: Implantation of the blastocyst into the endometrium occurs between day 7 and 8, finishing around day 14.
questions.imageAlt: Timeline of fertilization, cleavage and implantation

questions.question: Which important feature is TRUE of a mature red blood cell?
options: It has a large central nucleus
It is produced in the liver
It has no nucleus and is flexible
questions.explanation: Mature RBCs have no nucleus, are small and flexible, and live about 120 days.

questions.question: The oxygen-carrying molecule that gives red blood cells their colour is:
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin binds oxygen and gives RBCs their red colour.
questions.imageAlt: Oxygen binding to haemoglobin in red blood cells

questions.question: The normal lifespan of a red blood cell is approximately:
questions.explanation: Red blood cells live about 120 days before being destroyed in the liver and spleen.

questions.question: A reduction in red blood cell count leads to the condition known as:
questions.explanation: A reduced RBC count results in anaemia.

questions.question: The main function of leucocytes (white blood cells) is to:
options: Protect against bacteria, viruses, parasites and toxins
questions.explanation: White blood cells protect the body from harmful bacteria, viruses, parasites and toxic substances.
questions.imageAlt: The five main types of white blood cell

questions.question: Platelets (thrombocytes) are best described as:
questions.explanation: Platelets are cell fragments made in red bone marrow that promote blood clotting.

questions.question: Approximately what percentage of plasma is water?
questions.explanation: Plasma is a clear liquid that is about 92% water.

questions.question: Which plasma proteins increase osmotic pressure and include clotting factors and antibodies?
questions.explanation: Plasma proteins such as fibrinogen and antibodies raise osmotic pressure.

questions.question: On average, about how many times per minute does the heart beat?
questions.explanation: The heart pumps about 70 times per minute.

questions.question: The heart is located in the thorax:
options: Entirely on the right of the midline
On the superior surface of the diaphragm, left of the midline
questions.explanation: The heart sits on the superior surface of the diaphragm, left of the midline, posterior to the sternum.
questions.imageAlt: Location of the heart in the thoracic cavity

questions.question: The double-walled sac that surrounds the heart is the:
questions.explanation: The pericardium is a double-walled sac surrounding the heart and roots of the great vessels.

questions.question: The visceral layer of the serous pericardium is also known as the:
questions.explanation: The visceral layer of the serous pericardium is the epicardium, lining the heart surface.

questions.question: The muscular layer forming the bulk of the heart wall is the:
questions.explanation: The myocardium is the cardiac muscle layer forming the bulk of the heart wall.

questions.question: Which is NOT a function of the pericardium?
questions.explanation: The pericardium protects, anchors and prevents overfilling; the heartbeat is generated by the SA node.

questions.question: The receiving chambers of the heart are the:
questions.explanation: The atria are the receiving chambers; the ventricles are the discharging chambers.
questions.imageAlt: Internal chambers and valves of the heart

questions.question: Blood enters the left atrium from the:
questions.explanation: The left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins.

questions.question: Muscular ridges called pectinate muscles mark the walls of the:
questions.explanation: Pectinate muscles mark the atrial walls.
questions.imageAlt: Atria and pectinate muscles of the heart

questions.question: Papillary muscles and trabeculae carneae are found in the:
questions.explanation: Papillary muscles and trabeculae carneae (muscular ridges) mark the ventricular walls.
questions.imageAlt: Ventricles with papillary muscles and trabeculae carneae

questions.question: Why is the wall of the left ventricle the thickest chamber wall?
options: It must pump blood into the systemic circulation
It receives blood from the lungs
questions.explanation: The left ventricle wall is thickest because it pumps blood into the high-resistance systemic circulation.

questions.question: The valve located between the right atrium and right ventricle is the:
questions.explanation: The tricuspid (right AV) valve lies between the right atrium and right ventricle.

questions.question: The atrioventricular valves are anchored to the papillary muscles by the:
questions.explanation: Chordae tendineae anchor the AV valves to the papillary muscles, preventing backflow.

questions.question: The aortic semilunar valve lies between the:
questions.explanation: The aortic semilunar valve lies between the left ventricle and the aorta.

questions.question: In the pathway of blood through the heart, blood leaving the right ventricle passes through the pulmonary semilunar valve into the:
questions.explanation: The right ventricle pumps blood through the pulmonary semilunar valve into the pulmonary arteries toward the lungs.

questions.question: Cardiac muscle cells are joined end-to-end by structures that anchor cells and allow ion passage, called:
questions.explanation: Intercalated discs interconnect cardiac cells and allow free passage of ions.
questions.imageAlt: Intercalated disc with gap junctions and desmosomes

questions.question: Within intercalated discs, which junctions propagate action potentials between cardiac cells?
questions.explanation: Gap junctions link cardiac cells electrically to propagate action potentials; desmosomes convey contractile force.

questions.question: Because cardiac muscle acts as a coordinated unit, it is described as a functional:
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle behaves as a functional syncytium — cells contract as a single unit.

questions.question: The natural pacemaker of the heart is the:
questions.explanation: The SA node creates the excitation signal (action potential) that initiates each heartbeat.
questions.imageAlt: The conduction system of the heart

questions.question: In the conduction system, the electrical signal is briefly DELAYED at the:
questions.explanation: The signal is delayed at the AV node, allowing the atria to finish contracting before the ventricles.

questions.question: Which structure spreads the impulse rapidly through the ventricular walls, causing them to contract?
questions.explanation: The bundle of His and Purkinje fibres spread the impulse through the ventricles, causing contraction.

questions.question: The two coronary arteries that supply the heart arise from the:
options: Aortic sinuses (first branches of the aorta)
questions.explanation: The right and left coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses — the first branches of the aorta.
questions.imageAlt: Coronary arteries of the heart

questions.question: The left anterior descending artery mainly supplies the:
options: Anterior 2/3 of the interventricular septum and adjoining left ventricle
questions.explanation: The LAD supplies the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum and adjoining left ventricular wall.

questions.question: Most of the venous blood from the heart muscle drains into the right atrium via the:
questions.explanation: Cardiac veins join to form the coronary sinus, which opens into the right atrium.
questions.imageAlt: Coronary sinus and cardiac veins

questions.question: The largest vein of the heart, about 3 cm long and lying in the posterior coronary sulcus, is the:
questions.explanation: The coronary sinus is the largest cardiac vein (~3 cm) in the posterior coronary sulcus.

questions.question: Sympathetic nerve stimulation of the heart tends to:
options: Increase heart rate and dilate coronary arteries
questions.explanation: Sympathetic (cardio-acceleratory) nerves increase heart rate and dilate coronary arteries.

questions.question: Parasympathetic supply to the heart is carried mainly by which nerve?
questions.explanation: Parasympathetic (cardio-inhibitory) fibres run via the vagus nerve, slowing the heart rate.

questions.question: The hepatic portal circulation carries blood from the:
questions.explanation: The hepatic portal system carries partially deoxygenated, nutrient-rich blood from the gut to the liver.
questions.imageAlt: Hepatic portal circulation

questions.question: Angina pectoris and acute myocardial infarction are features of which condition?
questions.explanation: Angina pectoris and myocardial infarction are manifestations of ischaemic heart disease.

questions.question: A congenital narrowing of the aorta is termed:
questions.explanation: Coarctation of the aorta is a congenital narrowing of the aorta.
questions.imageAlt: Coarctation (narrowing) of the aorta

questions.question: Congestive heart failure may result from all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Congestive heart failure follows reduced contractility, diminished coronary flow or damaged valves.

questions.question: An atrial septal defect is classified as a:
questions.explanation: Atrial septal defect is a congenital abnormality of the heart.

questions.question: Functionally, the respiratory system is divided into a conducting portion and a:
questions.explanation: The system has a conducting portion (transports air) and a respiratory portion (gas exchange).
questions.imageAlt: Overview of the respiratory system

questions.question: Which of the following belongs to the RESPIRATORY (gas-exchange) portion?
questions.explanation: The respiratory portion includes respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts and alveoli.

questions.question: The medial wall of the nasal cavity (nasal septum) is formed partly by the perpendicular plate of the:
questions.explanation: The nasal septum is formed by the perpendicular plate of the ethmoid, the vomer and septal cartilage.
questions.imageAlt: Sagittal view of the nasal cavity

questions.question: The main function of the nasal conchae is to:
options: Increase the surface area of the nasal cavity
questions.explanation: The conchae increase surface area so inspired air is warmed and humidified.

questions.question: Most of the nasal cavity is lined by respiratory mucosa consisting of:
options: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with goblet cells
questions.explanation: Respiratory mucosa is pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with mucus-secreting goblet cells.
questions.imageAlt: Respiratory mucosa: ciliated cells and goblet cells

questions.question: The olfactory mucosa of the nasal cavity is specialised to contain:
questions.explanation: The olfactory mucosa contains smell receptors.

questions.question: How many paired paranasal sinuses are there?
questions.explanation: There are four paired paranasal sinuses: maxillary, frontal, sphenoid and ethmoid.
questions.imageAlt: The paranasal sinuses

questions.question: Inflammation of the paranasal sinuses is called:
questions.explanation: Sinusitis is inflammation of the paranasal sinuses.

questions.question: The pharynx is divided into three parts. Which is the correct set?
questions.explanation: The three parts of the pharynx are the nasopharynx, oropharynx and laryngopharynx.
questions.imageAlt: The three regions of the pharynx

questions.question: The pharyngeal tonsil is located in the:
questions.explanation: The pharyngeal tonsil is located in the nasopharynx.

questions.question: The oropharynx and laryngopharynx are both lined by:
questions.explanation: Both are lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium to withstand friction from food and air.

questions.question: The largest laryngeal cartilage, forming the 'Adam's apple', is the:
questions.explanation: The thyroid cartilage forms the Adam's apple and gives anterior attachment to the vocal folds.
questions.imageAlt: The larynx and epiglottis

questions.question: Which laryngeal cartilage is ring-shaped and made of hyaline cartilage?
questions.explanation: The cricoid cartilage is a complete ring of hyaline cartilage.
questions.imageAlt: Cartilages of the larynx (anterior view)

questions.question: The single fibroelastic cartilage that prevents food entering the airway during swallowing is the:
questions.explanation: The epiglottis is a fibroelastic cartilage that closes off the airway when swallowing.

questions.question: The trachea is supported by how many incomplete (C-shaped) rings of hyaline cartilage?
questions.explanation: The trachea has 16 to 20 incomplete C-shaped rings of hyaline cartilage.

questions.question: The free ends of the tracheal cartilage rings are connected posteriorly by the:
questions.explanation: The trachealis (smooth) muscle joins the free ends of the C-rings and adjusts tracheal diameter.

questions.question: The internal ridge where the trachea divides into the two primary bronchi is called the:
questions.explanation: The carina is the internal ridge formed by the most inferior tracheal cartilage at the bronchial split.
questions.imageAlt: Trachea, carina and bronchial branching

questions.question: The trachea is lined by which type of epithelium?
questions.explanation: The trachea is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium.

questions.question: Why are inhaled foreign bodies more likely to lodge in the RIGHT primary bronchus?
options: It is shorter, wider and more vertical than the left
It is longer and more horizontal
questions.explanation: The right primary bronchus is shorter, wider and more vertically oriented, so aspirated objects lodge there more easily.
questions.imageAlt: The bronchial tree

questions.question: How many secondary (lobar) bronchi does the RIGHT lung have?
questions.explanation: The right lung has three secondary (lobar) bronchi; the left lung has two.

questions.question: Air-conducting tubes smaller than about 1 mm in diameter are called:
questions.explanation: Tubes smaller than 1 mm are bronchioles; the smallest terminal bronchioles are under 0.5 mm.

questions.question: As the airways become smaller toward the terminal bronchioles, the epithelium changes to:
questions.explanation: The epithelium grades from pseudostratified ciliated columnar to simple cuboidal in terminal bronchioles.

questions.question: Sympathetic stimulation of bronchial smooth muscle causes:
questions.explanation: Sympathetic stimulation relaxes bronchial smooth muscle (bronchodilation); parasympathetic causes constriction.

questions.question: Which structure serves as the transition between the conducting and respiratory portions?
questions.explanation: Respiratory bronchioles are the transition between the conducting and respiratory portions.
questions.imageAlt: Terminal and respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts and alveoli

questions.question: The tiny sac-like outpocketings where gas exchange occurs are the:
questions.explanation: Alveoli are thin-walled sacs specialised for gas exchange with pulmonary capillaries.

questions.question: Approximately how many alveoli does each lung contain?
questions.explanation: Each lung contains approximately 300–400 million alveoli.

questions.question: The respiratory membrane, across which gases diffuse, is about how thick?
questions.explanation: The respiratory membrane is only about 0.5 µm thick, easing gas diffusion.
questions.imageAlt: Gas exchange between alveoli and capillaries

questions.question: The thin, simple squamous cells that form most of the alveolar lining are:
questions.explanation: Type I (squamous) cells form the alveolar lining across which gases diffuse.
questions.imageAlt: Structure of an alveolus

questions.question: Surfactant, which reduces surface tension in the alveoli, is secreted by:
questions.explanation: Type II cuboidal cells secrete surfactant, a detergent-like substance.

questions.question: The main role of pulmonary surfactant is to:
options: Reduce surface tension and prevent alveolar collapse during expiration
questions.explanation: Surfactant lowers surface tension, preventing alveolar walls from collapsing during expiration.

questions.question: Difficulty breathing in premature babies is largely due to a lack of:
questions.explanation: Premature babies often lack surfactant, causing breathing difficulty.

questions.question: The membrane that covers the outer surface of the lungs is the:
questions.explanation: The visceral pleura covers the lungs; the parietal pleura lines the chest wall.
questions.imageAlt: Pleural layers and pleural cavity

questions.question: The pleural fluid in the pleural cavity functions mainly to:
options: Act as a lubricant reducing friction during breathing
questions.explanation: Serous pleural fluid lubricates the pleural surfaces, minimising friction during breathing.

questions.question: The right lung is divided into how many lobes?
questions.explanation: The right lung has three lobes (oblique and horizontal fissures); the left has two.
questions.imageAlt: Lobes and fissures of the lungs

questions.question: The deep notch on the anterior border of the LEFT lung that accommodates the heart is the:
questions.explanation: The cardiac notch on the left lung's anterior border accommodates the apex of the heart.

questions.question: The only site where structures enter or exit the lung is the:
questions.explanation: The hilum on the medial aspect is the only site of entry/exit for vessels, bronchi and nerves.

questions.question: The wide, concave base of each lung rests upon the:
questions.explanation: The concave base of each lung rests upon the muscular diaphragm.

questions.question: The structural and functional units of lung parenchyma, numbering about ten in the right lung, are the:
questions.explanation: Bronchopulmonary segments (about 10 on the right) are the structural/functional units of lung tissue.

questions.question: The right lung is located slightly more superiorly than the left because of the:
options: Liver lying below on the right side
questions.explanation: The liver on the right side pushes the right lung slightly more superiorly.

questions.question: The upper (vestibule) region of the nasal cavity, which filters large particles, is lined with:
options: Skin containing hairs and sebaceous/sweat glands
questions.explanation: The vestibule is lined with skin bearing nose hairs and sebaceous/sweat glands to filter large particles.

questions.question: Which of the following is an ACCESSORY organ rather than part of the GI tract itself?
questions.explanation: The liver (with the pancreas and gall bladder) is an accessory organ; the others are parts of the GI tract.
questions.imageAlt: Organs of the digestive system

questions.question: The wall of the alimentary tract is formed by how many layers of tissue?
questions.explanation: The tract wall has four layers: mucosa, submucosa, muscle layer and adventitia/serosa.
questions.imageAlt: Histological cross-section of the digestive tract

questions.question: From the lumen outward, the correct order of the tract wall layers is:
questions.explanation: From the lumen: mucosa (innermost), submucosa, muscularis, then serosa/adventitia (outermost).

questions.question: The muscle layer of the tract typically consists of an inner circular layer and an outer:
questions.explanation: The muscularis has an inner circular and an outer longitudinal layer of smooth muscle.

questions.question: The myenteric (Auerbach's) plexus that controls peristalsis lies between the:
questions.explanation: The myenteric (Auerbach's) plexus lies between the circular and longitudinal muscle layers and controls peristalsis.

questions.question: The submucosal nerve plexus that supplies the mucosal lining is called:
questions.explanation: The submucosal (Meissner's) plexus supplies the mucosal lining.

questions.question: The serous membrane covering the abdominal organs is the:
questions.explanation: In the abdomen the organs are covered by a serous membrane called the peritoneum.
questions.imageAlt: Parietal and visceral peritoneum

questions.question: The peritoneal layer that covers the abdominal viscera is the:
questions.explanation: The visceral peritoneum covers the abdominal viscera; the parietal peritoneum lines the abdominal wall.

questions.question: The greater omentum connects the greater curvature of the stomach to the:
questions.explanation: The greater omentum connects the greater curvature of the stomach to the transverse colon.

questions.question: The oesophagus and anal canal are lined mainly by which protective epithelium?
questions.explanation: The mouth, pharynx, oesophagus and anal canal are lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.

questions.question: The stomach and intestines are lined by which epithelium suited to secretion and absorption?
questions.explanation: Simple columnar epithelium lines the stomach and intestines and functions in secretion and absorption.

questions.question: Which salivary gland is purely serous and lies in front of the ear?
questions.explanation: The parotid gland is a serous gland situated in front of the ear.
questions.imageAlt: The salivary glands

questions.question: The parotid duct opens into the vestibule near the:
questions.explanation: The parotid duct opens into the vestibule near the second upper molar tooth.

questions.question: The lower one-third of the oesophagus contains which type of muscle?
questions.explanation: The upper 1/3 is voluntary muscle and the lower 1/3 is smooth muscle, with a gradual change in the middle.

questions.question: The lower oesophageal sphincter functions to:
options: Prevent reflux of acidic gastric contents
questions.explanation: The lower oesophageal sphincter prevents reflux of acid gastric content into the oesophagus.

questions.question: At the gastro-oesophageal junction, the epithelium changes from stratified squamous to:
questions.explanation: At the junction, oesophageal stratified squamous mucosa changes to gastric simple columnar mucosa.

questions.question: The three main regions of the stomach are the fundus, body and:
questions.explanation: The stomach's parts are the fundus, body and pylorus.
questions.imageAlt: Parts and curvatures of the stomach

questions.question: Unlike the rest of the tract, the stomach muscularis has an ADDITIONAL layer, namely the:
questions.explanation: The stomach has three muscle layers — outer longitudinal, middle circular and an inner oblique layer for churning.
questions.imageAlt: Muscle layers of the stomach wall

questions.question: The folds of the stomach mucosa and submucosa seen when it is empty are called:
questions.explanation: Rugae are folds in the stomach mucosa and submucosa present when the stomach is empty.

questions.question: Which gastric cell type secretes hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor?
questions.explanation: Parietal cells secrete hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor (needed for vitamin B12 absorption).
questions.imageAlt: Gastric glands and their cells

questions.question: Chief cells of the gastric glands secrete:
questions.explanation: Chief cells secrete pepsinogen, the inactive precursor of pepsin.

questions.question: Intrinsic factor, secreted by parietal cells, is required for the absorption of:
questions.explanation: Intrinsic factor is required for absorption of vitamin B12.

questions.question: In which region does the greatest amount of digestion and absorption occur?
questions.explanation: The small intestine is the site of greatest digestion and absorption of nutrients and water.

questions.question: The correct order of the three divisions of the small intestine is:
questions.explanation: From the stomach: duodenum (~25 cm), jejunum (~2.5 m), then ileum (~3.5 m).

questions.question: The absorptive surface area of the small intestine is increased by villi, microvilli and:
questions.explanation: Villi, microvilli and the permanent circular folds (plica circularis) all enlarge the absorptive surface.
questions.imageAlt: Villi, crypts, enterocytes and microvilli of the small intestine

questions.question: The columnar absorptive cells covering the villi are called:
questions.explanation: Enterocytes are the columnar epithelial cells of the villi, bearing microvilli for absorption.

questions.question: Brunner's glands, which protect the mucosa from acid, are located in the submucosa of the:
questions.explanation: Brunner's glands are mucous glands in the submucosa of the duodenum, neutralising acidic chyme.

questions.question: Collections of lymphatic tissue called Peyer's patches are found mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Peyer's patches (aggregated lymphatic follicles) are found mainly in the ileum.
questions.imageAlt: Plica circularis and aggregated lymphatic follicle (Peyer's patch)

questions.question: Which is the correct order of the sections of the large intestine?
options: Caecum, ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid colon
questions.explanation: The large intestine runs caecum → ascending → transverse → descending → sigmoid colon.
questions.imageAlt: Sections of the large intestine

questions.question: Inflammation of the vermiform appendix is termed:
questions.explanation: Appendicitis is inflammation of the appendix, a blind tube projecting from the caecum.

questions.question: The three thickened bands of longitudinal muscle characteristic of the large intestine are the:
questions.explanation: The longitudinal muscle of the colon is gathered into three bands called taeniae coli.
questions.imageAlt: Taenia coli and layers of the colon

questions.question: The external anal sphincter is composed of which muscle type?
questions.explanation: The external anal sphincter is skeletal muscle under voluntary control; the internal sphincter is smooth muscle.

questions.question: The exocrine portion of the pancreas is made of glandular cell clusters called:
questions.explanation: Exocrine pancreatic acini secrete pancreatic juice containing digestive enzymes.
questions.imageAlt: The pancreas: head, body and tail

questions.question: Which pancreatic islet cells secrete insulin?
questions.explanation: Beta cells of the islets of Langerhans secrete insulin.
questions.imageAlt: Endocrine pancreas: islet of Langerhans cells

questions.question: Alpha (α) cells of the pancreatic islets secrete:
questions.explanation: Alpha cells secrete glucagon; beta cells secrete insulin; delta cells secrete somatostatin.

questions.question: The largest gland in the body, weighing about 1500 g, is the:
questions.explanation: The liver is the largest gland in the body, weighing about 1500 g.
questions.imageAlt: Anatomical lobes of the liver

questions.question: The fibrous capsule covering the liver is known as:
questions.explanation: The liver is covered by a fibrous layer called Glisson's capsule.

questions.question: The liver receives about 80% of its blood supply (partially deoxygenated but nutrient-rich) from the:
questions.explanation: About 80% of hepatic blood is partially deoxygenated portal venous blood; ~20% is oxygenated arterial blood.

questions.question: At the periphery of each liver lobule, the portal triad consists of a bile duct, a venule and a(n):
questions.explanation: The portal triad contains an arteriole (hepatic artery branch), a venule (portal vein branch) and a bile duct.

questions.question: The gall bladder is a pear-shaped sac attached to the posterior surface of the liver, with three parts: fundus, body and:
questions.explanation: The gall bladder has three parts: fundus, body and neck.
questions.imageAlt: Parts of the gall bladder and bile ducts
